content.text: A robot that does not know where it is cannot plan where to go. Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) solves the chicken-and-egg problem at the heart of autonomous navigation: you need a map to localise, and you need your position to build a map. SLAM algorithms solve both problems together, incrementally constructing a globally consistent map of the environment while simultaneously tracking the robot's pose within that map — all in real time, without pre-existing infrastructure.

SLAM is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's location within it. The map may be a 2D occupancy grid, a 3D point cloud, a set of 3D landmarks, or a dense mesh. The pose estimate is typically a 6-DOF transform (position x,y,z plus orientation roll, pitch, yaw) in the map frame.
Localization, when a map already exists, is the simpler subproblem of answering "where am I in this map?" using current sensor readings. The gold-standard ROS 2 localizer for a known static map is
(Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization), a particle filter that maintains a probabilistic distribution of pose hypotheses and weights them against LiDAR scan observations.

GPS is unavailable indoors, unreliable underground, and insufficiently precise for centimetre-level navigation even outdoors. SLAM enables autonomous robots to operate in GPS-denied environments — factories, hospitals, mines, subterranean survey sites, planetary surfaces — without any pre-installed beacons or markers. A new robot dropped into an unknown environment can explore, build a map, and begin delivering items within minutes using only its onboard sensors.

Accumulated drift is the fundamental challenge SLAM must overcome. Odometry — integrating wheel encoder measurements to estimate displacement — drifts without bound over time. A robot travelling 100 m using pure odometry may have an accumulated position error of 1–3 m. SLAM corrects this by matching new sensor observations back to previously seen landmarks or scan segments, providing absolute position corrections that keep the map and pose globally consistent.
Infrastructure-free operation: no GPS, no markers, no pre-surveyed environment required.
Global consistency: loop closure corrects accumulated drift, producing maps that are accurate at large scale.
Incrementally online: the map is usable immediately and grows as the robot explores, without waiting for a batch post-processing step.
Dual output: both the map (for planning) and the pose (for navigation) emerge from the same algorithm.
Applicable to diverse sensors: LiDAR SLAM, visual SLAM, acoustic SLAM, and RGB-D SLAM all share the same probabilistic framework.
Computationally expensive at large scale: maintaining a global covariance matrix over all landmarks is O(n²); sparse factor graphs are essential for large environments.
Loop closure is hard: symmetric or featureless environments (long corridors, white rooms) make it difficult to detect a revisited location.
Dynamic objects degrade SLAM: people, vehicles, and moving furniture are inconsistencies in the map that must be detected and excluded.
Visual SLAM fails in darkness, direct sunlight, and texture-less environments; LiDAR SLAM fails in feature-poor spaces (open fields, empty warehouses).
Tuning required: scan matching convergence thresholds, loop closure descriptor parameters, and optimiser noise models must be tuned per deployment.
Modern SLAM systems are built on

. A factor graph is a bipartite graph in which variable nodes (robot poses x₀, x₁, ..., xₙ and landmark positions l₁, ..., lₘ) are connected to factor nodes (motion factors from odometry, observation factors from sensor measurements, and loop closure factors). The SLAM problem becomes a nonlinear least-squares optimisation over all variable nodes:

is the information matrix (inverse covariance) weighting each factor. Solvers like GTSAM's iSAM2, g2o, and Ceres minimise this sum using sparse Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt iterations, exploiting the sparsity of the factor graph for efficiency.

Loop closure detection is the mechanism by which a SLAM system recognises that it has returned to a previously visited location. For LiDAR SLAM, this uses scan-to-map matching (ICP or NDT): a newly acquired scan is aligned against the accumulated map; if the match residual is below a threshold, a loop closure factor is added to the graph and all poses are re-optimised. For visual SLAM, bag-of-words image retrieval (DBoW2, VLAD) is used to find similar historical keyframes, followed by geometric verification with essential matrix or PnP.
The two dominant SLAM paradigms are

. LiDAR SLAM (Cartographer, LIO-SAM, LeGO-LOAM) operates on 3D point clouds and is robust to lighting changes but requires a spinning LiDAR (expensive) and fails in open fields. Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM3, DSO, RTAB-Map) operates on camera images and is inexpensive but degrades in low texture and low light. Fusing both with an IMU (LiDAR-inertial, visual-inertial SLAM) covers a much wider operating envelope.

attrs.alt: SLAM factor graph showing pose nodes connected by odometry factors, landmark observations, and a loop closure edge, feeding into graph optimizer and AMCL localizer

content.text: Pose nodes x₀–x₅ are linked by odometry factors (amber). Observation factors (purple) connect poses to landmark nodes when a landmark is detected. The loop closure factor (red dashed arc) links the first and last pose when the robot recognises it has returned to the start. The entire graph is optimised jointly, and the result feeds AMCL for ongoing localisation.
Initial deployment in a new facility: SLAM generates the map; switch to AMCL localisation once the map is stable.
GPS-denied environments: underground mines, indoor warehouses, search-and-rescue scenarios.
Environments that change over time: re-running SLAM periodically generates an updated map reflecting structural changes.
Multi-robot exploration: each robot runs SLAM independently and maps are merged via multi-session map merging.
The standard ROS 2 SLAM stack uses

(for 2D LiDAR SLAM) or Google Cartographer (for 2D/3D). LIO-SAM provides tightly coupled LiDAR-inertial SLAM for 3D environments. ORB-SLAM3 handles monocular, stereo, and RGB-D visual SLAM. After mapping, the map is saved with
and AMCL is launched for ongoing localisation. The
manages bringup of the full localisation and navigation stack.

# 1. Launch a simulated TurtleBot4 with Cartographer 2D SLAM in ROS 2 Humble
#    Requires: turtlebot4_simulator, slam_toolbox, nav2_bringup

# Terminal 1: start simulation
ros2 launch turtlebot4_ignition_bringup ignition.launch.py

# Terminal 2: start SLAM with slam_toolbox (online, asynchronous mode)
ros2 launch slam_toolbox online_async_launch.py \
    slam_params_file:=./config/mapper_params_online_async.yaml \
    use_sim_time:=true

# Terminal 3: start Nav2 navigation (SLAM mode — no prior map)
ros2 launch nav2_bringup navigation_launch.py \
    use_sim_time:=true \
    autostart:=true \
    params_file:=./config/nav2_params.yaml

# Terminal 4: explore with teleop to build the map
ros2 run teleop_twist_keyboard teleop_twist_keyboard

# When mapping is complete, save the map
ros2 run nav2_map_server map_saver_cli \
    -f ~/maps/my_map \
    --ros-args -p save_map_timeout:=5.0
# Produces my_map.yaml and my_map.pgm

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Switch to localisation-only mode (AMCL) with the saved map
# -------------------------------------------------------------------

# Terminal 2 replacement: launch AMCL localiser with saved map
ros2 launch nav2_bringup localization_launch.py \
    map:=~/maps/my_map.yaml \
    use_sim_time:=true

# Provide an initial pose estimate (RViz2 "2D Pose Estimate" button, or CLI)
ros2 topic pub /initialpose geometry_msgs/msg/PoseWithCovarianceStamped \
    '{header: {frame_id: map}, pose: {pose: {
      position: {x: 0.0, y: 0.0, z: 0.0},
      orientation: {w: 1.0}}}}' \
    --once

"""Minimal factor graph SLAM with GTSAM: 1D example fusing odometry
and two absolute GPS measurements to correct drift."""
import gtsam
from gtsam import symbol_shorthand
import numpy as np

X = symbol_shorthand.X  # pose symbols x0, x1, x2, ...

def run_slam():
    graph = gtsam.NonlinearFactorGraph()
    initial = gtsam.Values()

    # Noise models
    odom_noise  = gtsam.noiseModel.Diagonal.Sigmas(np.array([0.1, 0.1, 0.02]))  # x, y, theta
    prior_noise = gtsam.noiseModel.Diagonal.Sigmas(np.array([0.01, 0.01, 0.001]))
    gps_noise   = gtsam.noiseModel.Diagonal.Sigmas(np.array([0.5, 0.5]))

    # Prior on first pose (known start position)
    prior_pose = gtsam.Pose2(0.0, 0.0, 0.0)
    graph.add(gtsam.PriorFactorPose2(X(0), prior_pose, prior_noise))
    initial.insert(X(0), prior_pose)

    # Odometry measurements (simulating forward + turn)
    odom_steps = [
        gtsam.Pose2(1.0, 0.0, 0.0),
        gtsam.Pose2(1.0, 0.0, np.pi/4),
        gtsam.Pose2(1.0, 0.0, 0.0),
        gtsam.Pose2(1.0, 0.0, -np.pi/4),
    ]
    for i, delta in enumerate(odom_steps):
        graph.add(gtsam.BetweenFactorPose2(X(i), X(i+1), delta, odom_noise))
        # Initialise with dead-reckoning
        prev = initial.atPose2(X(i))
        initial.insert(X(i+1), prev.compose(delta))

    # GPS observation at pose x2 and x4 (absolute position)
    gps_factor_model = gtsam.noiseModel.Diagonal.Sigmas(np.array([0.5, 0.5]))
    # GPS says x2 is at (1.7, 0.7) -- corrects drift
    graph.add(gtsam.GPSFactor2(X(2), gtsam.Point2(1.7, 0.7), gps_noise))
    graph.add(gtsam.GPSFactor2(X(4), gtsam.Point2(3.4, 0.4), gps_noise))

    # Optimise
    params = gtsam.LevenbergMarquardtParams()
    optimizer = gtsam.LevenbergMarquardtOptimizer(graph, initial, params)
    result = optimizer.optimize()

    print("Optimised poses:")
    for i in range(5):
        p = result.atPose2(X(i))
        print(f"  x{i}: ({p.x():.3f}, {p.y():.3f}, theta={np.degrees(p.theta()):.1f}deg)")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_slam()

Boston Dynamics' Spot robot uses an onboard SLAM system to navigate multi-floor commercial buildings for inspection tasks. The robot builds a 3D point cloud map on its first walk-through using its five stereo cameras in a visual-inertial SLAM configuration. On subsequent missions, it localises against the stored map using AMCL-like place recognition. Loop closures are detected when the robot re-enters a previously mapped area, correcting any accumulated drift. The system is robust enough to re-localise after a total shutdown and restart ("kidnapped robot" problem) by performing a global particle filter initialisation scan.
Running SLAM with an incorrect tf tree: if base_link → lidar transform is wrong, scan matching will always fail.
Moving too fast during map building: ICP/NDT scan matching requires sufficient overlap between consecutive scans; high speed produces gaps that break matching.
Not verifying loop closure quality: a false positive loop closure adds an incorrect constraint to the graph, warping the entire map. Always check the loop closure score threshold.
Using SLAM in a dynamic environment without dynamic object removal: moving people leave ghost trails in the map that block navigation paths.
Forgetting to save the map before shutdown: slam_toolbox's online mode stores the map in memory; serialise to disk explicitly.
Walk slowly and systematically during initial mapping — overlap each scan by at least 50% of the previous scan area.
Use IMU pre-integration as the motion prior for LiDAR SLAM (LIO-SAM style); it handles high-speed motion and elevator transitions far better than wheel odometry.
Evaluate map quality quantitatively using the Absolute Pose Error (APE) metric against ground truth if available.
Store the full factor graph (not just the occupancy grid) so that maps can be re-optimised offline if new loop closures are found later.
Set appropriate particle counts in AMCL: too few (200) degrades localisation in complex environments; too many (10 000) wastes CPU. Start with 500–2000 and adjust.

slam_toolbox in asynchronous mode processes at 5–10 Hz on a mid-range CPU for typical indoor environments up to a few thousand square metres. LIO-SAM processes a 64-beam LiDAR at 10 Hz with IMU pre-integration in under 50 ms per scan on a modern Intel i7, scaling to several-hundred-metre trajectories. ORB-SLAM3 tracks 500 ORB features per frame at 30 Hz on a GPU but degrades to 10 Hz on CPU for larger environments.

For large-scale environments (multi-kilometre outdoor survey), submapping strategies partition the environment into locally consistent submaps connected by inter-submap constraints — Cartographer's submap-based approach is the canonical solution. GTSAM's iSAM2 incrementally factors new constraints without re-solving the full graph, keeping optimisation time under 10 ms per new pose in graphs with thousands of nodes.
Online async + lifelong mapping modes; saves graph for re-use
Submap-based; handles large outdoor environments; ROS 2 port available
Tightly coupled IMU; excellent for outdoor/elevated environments
iSAM2 incremental solver; C++ and Python APIs
Adaptive particle count; beam model or likelihood field

SLAM reliability depends on the environment never becoming completely featureless. A mobile robot entering an entirely empty, white, cubical room will lose tracking because there are no visual or geometric features to match. Mitigation strategies include: maintaining an IMU dead-reckoning fallback for short outages, placing artificial landmarks (QR codes, ArUco markers) in known positions as a recovery mechanism, and implementing a kidnapped-robot detection that triggers a global particle filter reinitialisation when localisation diverges beyond a threshold.
SLAM solves the chicken-and-egg problem of map-building and localisation jointly, enabling robots to operate in completely unknown environments.
The factor graph formulation unifies odometry, observation, and loop closure constraints into a single nonlinear least-squares problem.
Loop closure is critical: without it, odometry drift makes the map geometrically inconsistent over large trajectories.
slam_toolbox and Cartographer are the production-ready 2D/3D LiDAR SLAM stacks for ROS 2; LIO-SAM adds tight IMU coupling for outdoor use.
AMCL is the standard Nav2 localiser for known static maps; use SLAM only when no prior map exists.
GTSAM's iSAM2 enables real-time incremental optimisation of large factor graphs, making scalable online SLAM practical.
A correct tf tree and slow systematic mapping walks are the two most impactful prerequisites for SLAM quality.
